original_abstract: The biological identity and ultimate physiological fate of nanoparticles (NPs) are dictated by the "protein corona," a layer of adsorbed biomolecules that forms immediately upon contact with physiological fluids. While the influence of NP physicochemical properties on corona composition is well documented, the impact of the specific biological source remains poorly understood. In this study, we hypothesize that alterations in the human plasma proteome caused by specific diseases significantly modify the resulting NP corona. To test this, we incubated hydrophobic polystyrene and hydrophilic silica nanoparticles with plasma samples obtained from subjects across a wide spectrum of medical conditions, including various cancers, diabetes, hypercholesterolemia, coagulation disorders (hemophilia and hypofibrinogenemia), and pregnancy. Using SDS-PAGE, dynamic light scattering, and zeta potential analysis, we demonstrate that the disease state of the donor serves as a critical determinant of the protein corona’s molecular signature. Our findings reveal that patients with distinct pathologies produce unique, identifiable coronas even when exposed to identical nanomaterials. Consequently, we propose the concept of the "personalized protein corona" (PPC) as a vital factor in the nanobiointerface. Integrating the PPC into the design and evaluation of nanomedicines is essential for the transition toward effective, patient-specific therapeutic and diagnostic applications.
humanized_abstract: The protein corona, a dynamic self-associating layer of adsorbed biological molecules, immediately forms on the surface of the nanoparticles upon exposure to the biological fluid environment thereby determining their biological identity and their ultimate physiological behavior. Although numerous research studies demonstrated that the bioavailability and structure of the protein corona is a function of the physical–chemical characteristics of the nanoparticle as well as the biochemistry of the biological environment, only limited insights have been achieved regarding the role of the nature of the biological environment. Here we investigate for the first time to our knowledge whether and how the alterations of the proteome of human plasma induced by diseases impacts the constitution of the corona, and if so, the extent of such alterations. Our investigation included the exposition of hydrophobic (polystyrene, PS) and hydrophilic (silica, SiO2) nanoparticles to the plasma of several human plasmas derived from patients under different therapeutic schedules and/or with severe diseases corresponding to various disease categories including cancer, diabetes, hypercholesterolemia as well as coagulation disorders like hemophilia and hypofibrinogenemia, pregnancy. Following the interaction of nanoparticles with human plasma derived from patients affected by several different and severe pathological conditions including as mentioned different cancers, diabetes, hypercholesterolemia, haemophilia, hypofibrinogenemia, pregnancy, analysis by SDS-PAGE, dynamic light scattering and zeta potential shown that a clear change in the identity of the protein corona on NPs is taking place depending exclusively on the disease of the donor human plasma. Therefore the patients affected by the different conditions, while all exposed to the same nanomaterials, form in their plasma upon nanoparticles exposure protein coronas whose composition significantly differs each other, highlighting the importance of what we propose to denominate the "personalized protein corona" (PPC) at the nanobiinterface, at least when developing new nano-therapeutic applications.
